figures.gloss: The supreme zemi — immaterial, invisible and immortal, dwelling in the sky, named for yuca (cassava), bagua (the sea) and his fatherless origin; he gives the staple manioc on which Taíno life depends. Pané's first sentence records that the Taíno held a being who 'had a mother but no father'.
figures.facet: A high god defined by a paradox of birth: the maker of the staple crop is himself motherborn-but-fatherless, sovereign yet so immaterial he is barely storied — provider of cassava and the sea, never seen, never slain, the withdrawn lord glimpsed only through what he feeds.
figures.provenance: Fray Ramón Pané, Relación acerca de las antigüedades de los indios (1498), ch. 1; name-elements reconstructed by Arrom and Stevens-Arroyo (Cave of the Jagua). The triple name is a scholarly compound; theological detail is sparse and inferred.

figures.gloss: Mother of Yúcahu and goddess of fresh water, the moon, fertility and childbirth — Pané lists her five names. She is the womb-of-waters from whom the high god is born without a father, ruler of the springs, rivers and the tides of woman and moon, invoked by women in labour.
figures.facet: The mother who outranks her own supreme son in priority of being: where many pantheons set a withdrawn father first, here the originating principle is explicitly maternal and watery — the sweet-water moon-mother who bears the sky-lord, fertility prior to sovereignty.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), ch. 1, who records the five names; her watery/lunar attributes are drawn together by Arrom and Stevens-Arroyo. The number of names is firmly attested; their full theology is reconstructed.

figures.gloss: The female zemi of the hurricane — 'lady of the winds' — who when angered looses the storm with two heralds: Guataubá, who goes before her marshalling the winds and thunder, and Coatrisquie, who gathers the waters and pours down the flood. The Spanish 'huracán' descends from this Antillean storm-complex.
figures.facet: Catastrophe gendered female and given a court: the destroying tempest is not a sky-father's bolt but a wrathful goddess with a thunder-herald and a flood-herald — the spiralling Antillean hurricane personified, its very whirl preserved in the word the world still uses for the storm.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), reported via Oviedo and Las Casas; the herald-pair Guataubá and Coatrisquie is attested. The etymology of 'hurricane' from Taíno/Carib hurakán is well established; her iconography is read into Antillean spiral cemí-petroglyphs.

figures.gloss: At once the spirit-being and the carved idol of wood, stone, bone, cotton or shell that houses it — a zemí is a living power resident in its object, fed cohoba and the first fruits, consulted in trance and answering aloud. Caciques owned and inherited them; some were said to speak, move, even flee.
figures.facet: The idol with a voice and an appetite: not a symbol of a god but the god's actual dwelling — a thing that must be fed, that answers, that can run away — divinity localised in matter so completely that the carving is the deity, the one sacred power made many through its vessels.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498) at length on zemís that speak and are fed; Las Casas and Oviedo describe the idols. Surviving wooden and stone cemís (the duho-seated and three-pointed forms) confirm the material practice; their precise animacy is read from Pané's accounts.

figures.gloss: The central rite: the behique (shaman) or cacique purges, then inhales powdered cohoba (Anadenanthera seeds) through a forked tube from a carved platform held by a zemí, and in the ensuing trance the spirit-world opens — the zemís speak, the future is asked, the sick diagnosed, the dead and the unborn consulted.
figures.facet: Vision delivered by a plant through the very body of the idol: the trance is not a free soul-flight outward but an oracle inhaled at the zemí's own dish, so that ecstasy and the speaking-statue are one act — the shaman breathes the god in to hear it answer.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498) and Las Casas describe the cohoba ritual and its forked snuffing-tubes; carved cohoba platforms, vomiting-spatulas and inhalers survive archaeologically across the Greater Antilles.

figures.gloss: The cave on the mountain Cauta from which the first humankind emerged onto the earth, set with a watchman, Mácocael. When some ventured out and were caught by the rising Sun, they were turned to stone, to trees, or to birds — and so the people came up into a world where the daylight could fix them in place.
figures.facet: Origin as a coming-up out of the rock, with the Sun as the price of leaving: humans climb from a cave like the Pueblo emergence, but here the first error is to be caught abroad at dawn — the Sun petrifies the unwary, so mortality and the daylit world begin in one stone-turning moment.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), ch. 1; the cave Cacibajagua and the watchman Mácocael are named, as is the Sun's transformation of those caught outside. Antillean caves bear cemí-petroglyphs consistent with cave-origin reverence; the narrative itself is from Pané alone.

figures.gloss: Twin zemis of the weather and sons of the cacique Mautiatihuel: Boinayel, 'son of the grey serpent', whose weeping brings the rain, and Márohu, 'cloudless', who holds the fair dry sky. As idols they were kept and prayed to for water in drought, the wet brother and the clear brother of the Antillean season.
figures.facet: Divine twins split not into striker and watcher but into wet and dry: the pair is the agricultural weather itself halved — the crying rain-giver and the cloud-clearer — so that the twinship governs the very water-cycle the cassava world lives or dies by.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), who names Boinayel and Márohu and their human father; their meteorological roles are drawn from his account. The reading of the names is reconstructed (Arrom).

figures.gloss: Lord of Coaybay, the land of the dead, named for the guava. The opía — the dead — hide by day and roam by night, eating guava and joining the living at feasts; you may know one because the dead have no navel. His realm is set off in some hidden quarter of the island, the country of the night-walking ancestors.
figures.facet: An underworld ruled by night and named for a fruit the dead eat: there is no judgement, no fiery pit — only the opía who keep to the dark, feast on guava, and rejoin the dance unseen but for a missing navel, so death is a nocturnal neighbour rather than a sentence below.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), ch. 12, on Coaybay, the guava-eating dead, the navel-test and their nightly mingling with the living. Maquetaurie Guayaba is named as its lord; the realm's nocturnal, unjudged character is fully Pané's.

figures.gloss: A zemí shaped like a dog, with four feet 'like a dog's', who watches over the dead and guides the opía toward Coaybay. Pané reports that this idol would slip from the house by night and run off into the lagoons and forests, and had to be hunted down and tied with cords — the keeper of the dead that itself keeps escaping.
figures.facet: The dog as both guard and guide of the dead, and an idol that runs away: the psychopomp is canine here, like the dog-headed Xolotl, but it is a wooden zemí that physically flees into the swamp by night — the soul-guide made an unruly object that must be leashed.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), ch. 20, on Opiyelguabirán the dog-zemi, its guardianship of the dead and its night-flights into the lagoons. The dog-and-dead association is explicit; the name's reading is reconstructed.

figures.gloss: Four fatherless brothers born together, led by scabby-skinned Deminán Caracaracol. Fleeing Yaya's house with the gourd that held his dead son's bones, they spilled it and from the flood came the sea and its fish; a hurled cassava-cake struck Deminán's back, swelling into a tumour from which they cut a living turtle-woman, the mother of their line.
figures.facet: Culture-heroes who make the sea and their own ancestress by misadventure: the ocean pours from a broken bone-gourd and the first woman is cut from a sore on a hero's back — origin as a chain of accidents and afflictions, the world and the lineage botched into being rather than decreed.
figures.provenance: Pané (1498), ch. 9–11, on Yaya and Yayael's bones, the gourd-flood that becomes the sea, and the turtle-woman cut from Deminán's back. The episodes are vivid in Pané but compressed and obscure; details are debated.

edges.note: Mother and son: Pané records that the supreme Yúcahu 'had a mother but no father', and that mother is Atabey — the watery moon-mother who bears the fatherless lord of cassava and the sea.
Within the one cosmos, the storm-goddess Guabancex is the destroyer set against Yúcahu's gift of the food-giving sea and cassava — the hurricane that unmakes the harvest the high god provides.
The cohoba snuff is inhaled at the zemí's own carved platform so that the idol may speak; rite and vessel are one act — the trance is how the zemí answers.
The dog-zemi Opiyelguabirán guards and guides the opía toward Coaybay, the night-land ruled by Maquetaurie Guayaba — psychopomp and underworld-sovereign of the same Taíno realm of the dead.

Two halves of Taíno origin in Pané: humankind emerges from the Cave of the Jagua, while the quadruplets' misadventure makes the sea and the first ancestress — the peopling and the world-shaping told in adjacent breaths.

The weather split three ways and gendered: the twin rain-and-fair-sky brothers govern the season's ordinary water where Guabancex looses its catastrophic excess — gentle weather against the goddess of the storm.
Yúcahu is himself the chief zemi, and like all zemís was housed in carved idols and fed; the high god is the supreme instance of the one sacred power resident in many vessels.
Humankind climbs up into the world from the Cave of the Jagua — the emergence pattern, here a coming-out-of-the-rock guarded by Mácocael where the Sun fixes those caught abroad.

Origin as ascent into this world rather than fabrication from above — the Taíno emerge from a cave as the Diné climb through stacked prior worlds; the emergence motif independently reached across the Americas.

Both are American beginnings where the first people arrive from elsewhere into this world, but the Taíno climb up from a cave while Sky-Woman falls from above — a loose structural rhyme, opposite in direction, flagged as a reach.

The spirit-vessel arrived at twice in the New World: the zemí is the carved idol the spirit inhabits and that is fed and answers, as the ixiptla is the body the teōtl 'puts on' — divinity localised in a material vessel, separately conceived.

One sacred power expressed through many vessels — the zemí as the spirit-in-its-idol rhymes with teōtl, the single divine force whose faces are the gods and whose vessels are the ixiptla; the one-through-many joint reached on both sides of the Caribbean.

Lords of the land of the dead in the same Mesoamerican-Caribbean world — Maquetaurie Guayaba over the night-roaming, guava-eating opía and Mictlāntēcuhtli over the four-year journey of Mictlan; underworld-sovereigns of unjudged, non-fiery realms.

Death-rulers of a hidden New-World underworld; the Taíno Coaybay of the navel-less dead and the Maya Xibalba of One Death and Seven Death, kindred in setting though Coaybay neither judges nor plays the killing-game.

Sovereigns of a realm of the dead named for or filled with a fruit of the dead — guava in Coaybay, the pomegranate that binds Persephone to Hades — the underworld marked by what is eaten there, independently imagined.

The dog as guide of the dead, reached twice in the New World: Opiyelguabirán the dog-zemi steering the opía to Coaybay and dog-headed Xōlōtl leading the sun and the dead through the Mexica underworld; the canine psychopomp, separately arrived at.

The dog-or-jackal shaped guardian of the dead — the Taíno dog-zemi and Egyptian Anubis — the canine set to watch over the passage out of life, a parallel of the psychopomp across wholly unrelated worlds.

Mothers of the waters from whom life's increase comes — Atabey of the sweet springs, moon and birth and Mama Qucha the mother-sea — the mother-of-waters joint reached on the Antillean and Andean sides of the Americas.

Great water-mothers who hold the cycle of life and increase — Atabey of fresh water, moon and childbirth and Sedna of the sea-game in her undersea house — the female deep as the source, two unconnected coastal worlds.

Antillean and Mexica masters of the storm-waters, but inverted in valence: where Tlāloc's rain is the giver of life and a paradise, Guabancex's whirling tempest is catastrophe — the same weather-power split toward fertility there and ruin here.

Storm-deities attended by a herald of thunder — Guabancex preceded by Guataubá who marshals the winds and thunder, as the Japanese thunder-god rides the tempest — the storm given a thunderous outrider, separately conceived.

The principled bringer of disorder within the cosmos — Guabancex's destroying hurricane set against the food-giving order, loosely echoing the Smoking Mirror's cosmic disruption; flagged as a reach across separate traditions.

Specialists who enter trance to consult the spirit-world for healing and the future — the behique inhaling cohoba at the zemí's dish and the angakkuq's soul-flight — the ecstatic-shaman reached in the Antilles and the Arctic alike.

The trance-journey to speak with the spirits — cohoba's plant-borne oracle answering the Siberian shaman's three-world flight; the same office of the soul-traveler, here delivered through snuff and the speaking idol.

A supreme sky-dwelling lord at the head of the cosmos — Yúcahu invisible above as the reconstructed Indo-European *Dyēus is the bright sky-father — but the Taíno god is a mother-born provider of cassava, so the parallel is faint and flagged as a reach.

Quadruplet/twinned culture-bearing ancestors at the origin of the lived world and its waters — the four Caracaracol brothers from whom the sea pours and the Dogon Nommo pairs of the primal waters; a loose parallel of the multiple first-ancestors, flagged as a reach.

American divine twins, but the Antillean pair is split into wet and dry weather rather than the Maya twins' shared adventure against death; a loose rhyme of the twin-archetype on opposite functions, flagged as a reach.

Culture-heroes whose own affliction makes the world's gifts — scabby Deminán from whose sore back the first ancestress is cut, the Feathered Serpent who burns himself to rise as the morning star; a faint parallel of the suffering culture-bringer, flagged as a reach.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Taíno, the Arawak-speaking peoples of the Greater Antilles — Hispaniola, Cuba, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, and the Bahamas — as it stood when Columbus arrived in 1492. It centered on zemís (cemís): both ancestral and nature spirit-powers and the carved idols that housed them. Shattered by conquest and disease within decades, it is known chiefly from a single early account and is claimed today by Taíno revival movements.
dossier.cosmos: A supreme sky-dwelling zemí, Yúcahu — invisible, immortal, and said to have 'a mother but no father' — gives cassava (yuca) and the sea; his mother is Atabey, goddess of fresh water, the moon, and childbirth. The female zemí Guabancex looses the hurricane (from whose Antillean name the word descends) with her thunder- and flood-heralds. Humankind emerged from a cave, Cacibajagua, and the dead — the opía, known by their missing navel — dwell in Coaybay under the lord Maquetaurie Guayaba, hiding by day and roaming by night to eat guava and mingle with the living.
dossier.practice: Sacred power ran through the zemís, owned and inherited by chiefs (caciques) and kept as figures of wood, stone, cotton, or shell that were fed, consulted, and said to speak. Its central rite was cohoba: the behique (shaman) or cacique purged and then inhaled a hallucinogenic snuff (from Anadenanthera seeds) through a forked tube from a zemí-held platform, entering trance to diagnose illness and consult the spirits. The ball-game (batey) and the areíto — communal song-dances recounting myth and genealogy — were also central acts.
dossier.canon: Oral, and almost entirely filtered through one text — the 'Relación acerca de las antigüedades de los indios' of Fray Ramón Pané (c. 1498), the first ethnography written in the Americas — supplemented by Peter Martyr, Las Casas, and Oviedo, and by surviving carved cemís and cohoba paraphernalia.
dossier.reflects: Divinity localized in matter — the idol that is not a symbol of a god but the god's own dwelling, a power that must be fed and that answers aloud.
sources: Fray Ramón Pané (José Juan Arrom, ed.), 'Relación acerca de las antigüedades de los indios' (c. 1498; crit. ed. 1974)
Antonio M. Stevens-Arroyo, 'Cave of the Jagua: The Mythological World of the Taínos' (1988)
Irving Rouse, 'The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus' (1992)
